content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 25,
1994
Gilbert Martinez
California Cable Television Association
1121 L Street, Suite 400
Sacramento, California 95814
Re:
Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-94-085
Dear Mr. Martinez:
We respond to your request for advice regarding application
of the postgovernment employment restrictions of the Political
Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
OUESTION
Does your previous employment with the California Public
Utilities Commission,
a state agency, trigger the postgovernment
employment restrictions of the Act?
CONCLUS ION
No.
Under the facts you have provided, your previous
employment with the California Public Utilities Commission would
not trigger the postgovernment employment restrictions of the Act.
FACTS
2
You have recently terminated your employment with the
California Public Utilities Commission
(“CPUC”),
a government
agency,
and are now employed with the California Cable Television
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
This advice is based on the facts set forth in an advice
letter previously prepared for you by the conflicts attorney of
the California Public Utilities Commission, dated February 7,
1994.
This letter will not confer immunity from any possible
enforcement action if the assumed facts are erroneous.
In its
advice giving function the Commission does not operate as a finder
of fact.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—94—085
Page
2
Association,
a private industry association.
Your previous
position with the
CPUC was as a “Staff Services Analyst-General”,
and you worked in the CPUC’s Office of Governmental Affairs.
You
worked primarily as a liaison to the Transportation Division of
the CPUC where you monitored legislation for bills relating to
transportation and sent such bills to the CPUC Legal Division for
analysis.
Additionally, you communicated with sponsors of bills
and responded to constituent concerns on transportation matters.
You did not make recommendations to the
CPUC about positions on
bills.
To the extent you may have analyzed bills, your work was
subject to significant substantive review by the head of the
Office of Governmental Affairs.
Your position was not designated
under the CPUC Conflict of Interest Code.
ANALYSIS
The postgovernment employment restrictions of the Act are
found at Sections 87400 through 87406.
Specific prohibitions are
found in Sections 87401,
87402 and 87406.
SECTIONS 87401 and 87402:
Under Sections 87401 and 87402,
a former state administrative
official,
may not represent or assist in representing any other
person for compensation before any court, state administrative
agency,
or any officer or employee thereof,
by formal appearance
or by any communication,
for the purpose of influencing any
judicial, quasi—judicial or other proceeding if the proceeding is
one in which the former state administrative official participated
while employed by the State of California,
and the proceeding is
one in which the State of California is a party or has a direct
and substantial interest.
Section 87400 of the Act defines “participated” as:
{T]o have taken part personally and
substantially through decision, approval,
disapproval,
formal written recommendation,
rendering advice on a substantial basis,
investigation or use of confidential
information as an officer or employee, but
excluding
approval, disapproval or rendering
of legal advisory opinions to departmental or
agency staff which do not involve a specific
party or parties.
Your former duties with the CPUC do not constitute
“participation” in a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding,
as the term is defined under the Act.
Accordingly, your future
activity is not restricted by the provisions of Sections 87401 and
87402 of the Act.


File No.
A—94--085
Page
3
SECTION 87406:
Under Section 87406(d)
of the Act,
a designated employee of a
state administrative agency, or an employee who made or
participated in making decisions which may have had a foreseeable
material effect on any financial interest,
may not, for a period
of one year following state employment, appear before or
communicate to any state agency for which that employee worked in
the prior twelve months for the purpose of:
influencing
administrative action; influencing any proceeding involving the
issuance,
amendment,
award or revocation of a permit, license,
grant or contract; or, influencing the sale or purchase of goods
or property.
As you were not a designated employee of the CPUC, the
provisions of Section 87406(d) are only applicable if your duties
as an employee included making or participation in the making of
decisions which may have had a foreseeable material effect on any
financial interest.
Under
2 California Code of Regulations Section l8700,
an
employee of a state agency makes a governmental decision when,
acting within his authority, he:
(1)
votes on a matter;
(2)
appoints a person;
(3) obligates or commits his agency to any
course of action;
(4) enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his agency; or
(5)
determines not to vote,
appoint a
person, obligate his agency,
or enter into any contractual
agreement, other than for reasons of conflict of interest
identified in accordance with agency regulations adopted pursuant
to Government Code Section 87300.
An employee of a state agency participates in the making of a
governmental decision when, acting within the authority of his
position,
he:
(1) negotiates, without significant substantive
review, with a governmental entity or private person regarding the
decision;
(2)
advises or makes recommendations to the decision—
maker, either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review,
by either:
(A)
conducting research or an
investigation, which requires the exercise of independent judgment
on the part of the employee, where the outcome of such research or
investigation will influence a decision; or
(B)
preparing or
presenting any report, analysis or opinion, which requires the
exercise of independent judgment on the part of the employee,
where the purpose of such report, analysis or opinion is to
influence a decision.
Although the Fair Political Practices Commission promulgated
regulation Section 18700 with reference to Section 87100 of the
Act, the regulation’s definitions are instructive for defining the
provisions of Section 87406 of the Act.


File No.
A—94—085
Page
4
Acts which are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual or
clerical are not considered making or participation in the making
of a governmental decision.
Your former duties as a “Staff Services Analyst—General” do
not constitute making or participating in making a governmental
decision.
The provisions of Section 87406 of the Act are not
applicable to you and, therefore,
do not restrict your
postgovernmental activities.
If you have any further questions or require additional
advice, please contact me at
(916)
322-5901., otherwise, you may
govern yourself in accordance with the contents of this letter.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell:
1
u
Counsel, Legal Division

not trigger the postgovernment employment restrictions of the Act.

QUESTION: Does your previous employment with the California Public
Utilities Commission,
a state agency, trigger the postgovernment
employment restrictions of the Act?

CONCLUSION: No.
Under the facts you have provided, your previous
employment with the California Public Utilities Commission would
not trigger the postgovernment employment restrictions of the Act.
